repo.description: Vim-fork focused on extensibility and usability
repo.name: neovim

body:
## Problem
`nvim_set_option_value` lacks features present in other methods of setting options. #38420

## Solution
Add `operation` field to `nvim_set_option_value`, which takes an enum of "set", "append", "prepend", or "remove". This allows it to function similar to `:set opt=`, `:set opt+=`, etc.

## Notes
- `set` is unnecessary, but I like it for making the logic very consistent
-  ~~Does not fix #39038. I didn't change any of the append, prepend, or remove logic. All that will need careful inspection to ensure consistency with the docs. I think that'll be easier to manage as a separate PR.~~ Using C logic should make these consistent.

close #38420
close #14888
close #15720
close #39038
close #18000

comment_data.body: VALIDATE_CON
I was hoping we could reuse the internal C code and throw out the Lua code. The Lua code is a bespoke reimplementation of the `:set` semantics...
lol, that makes more sense. I'm not sure why I read the issue this way.

could we avoid publicly exposing `nvim__merge_option_value` by adding another `opts` field (and/or operation kind) to `nvim_set_option_value` ? 

(not a high priority, but just wondering)

comment_data.body:
It's only there for those stupid infix operators because I wanted to avoid modifying the option value if somebody did something like
```lua
local x = vim.opt.listchars + 'space:-'
```
I guess we could add a `dry_run` flag to `nvim_set_option_value` and return what the merged option would be. I didn't like adding `nvim__merge_option_value`, but I didn't know how much we wanted to mess with the API.

Another option is something like
```lua
local old_val = vim.api.nvim_get_option_value(...)
local merged_val = vim.api.nvim_set_option_value(...)
vim.api.nvim_set_option_value(name, old_val, {})
return make_option(..., merged_val)
```
But with how confusing options are with all the scopes, I'm worried that could be error prone.

If you're okay with something like `dry_run` though, I think that would simplify things a bit.

comment_data.body: Ah, that's because I started allowing strings for number values. The error comes from `numberopt_get_newval` (based around the cmdline) rather than the API code. Guess the error isn't too helpful. Maybe in `assert_valid_value`, I can only allow strings for wildchar and wildcharm. I think those are the only number opts that need it. Then if an invalid string is passed to those, it would get `E474: Invalid argument`.

comment_data.body:
Well, it's cleaner. With this, I can use `get_option_newval` on both numbers and strings, leaving the existing logic intact in `src/nvim/option.c`. Unfortunately, I forgot one of the tests that made me split it out in the first place. It still doesn't quite work due to chained operations. `vim.opt.wildignore + '*.a' + '*.b'` will calculate `vim.opt.wildignore + '*.a'` first, but not update the value. Then when it tries to add `'*.b'`, `get_option_newval` still uses the real option value as the LHS, not the intermediate value we calculated. That's why separate logic is needed if you really want to do the infix ops correctly.

It can pass all the tests if we just get rid of `dry_run`, but then you run into the first issue I mentioned, where the infix ops act as assignments. Maybe we can also add an `lhs` option to `nvim_set_option_value` to resolve this, but then I'll have to break out the string and number logic again. Still maybe better than adding `nvim__merge_option_value`.

comment_data.body:
It's up to you... but since we already went in the `dry_run` direction I would just make the "chained operations" a TODO (plus some tests asserting the "wrong" behavior), and then we can see what the damage is later when we reintroduce `nvim__merge_option_value` (in a separate PR).

This is mostly to get a clear picture of which changes are involved with that last remaining detail (chained operations), after we have solved the main goal.

comment_data.body:
Note: I considered converting the return value to lua (string to table), but that does make it inconsistent with `nvim_get_option_value`, and maps don't necessarily iterate in the same order as the string representation. I don't think the order of map type options matters, but I could be wrong because there are a lot of options I don't know about.
I still think it would be nicer for consumers to get a table back for lists/maps.
let's explore that in a followup?
Sure, if you want. Do you also want me to look at changing the return type for nvim_get_option_value? That could cause a lot of breakage, so maybe there could be a flag.

Map style options (as listed in `runtime/lua/vim/_core/options.lua`) are:
- fillchars
- listchars
- winhighlight

I read through all the options and also found some weird options that are kind of a mix of map and other things:
- breakindentopt
- diffopt
- messagesopt

There's also mousescroll, which looks like a map, but I guess that just counts as a list style option.

In none of these cases does order appear to matter, so I think returning a Lua val from at least `nvim_set_option_value` would be fine.

> so I think returning a Lua val from at least `nvim_set_option_value` would be fine.

that's a good idea, want to leave that as a followup so we can get this merged? mind rebasing this meanwhile?

Yes, I'll followup.

There are a couple undotree test failures after rebasing, but that looks like flakiness because I also see one [here](https://github.com/neovim/neovim/actions/runs/27467044184/job/81191129246?pr=40217).
Actually, it was pretty easy to do. Just wasn't sure I should do it before. I added it onto this PR with the last commit. Felt simpler than making another PR that would change the return type.

title: feat(api): add operation field to nvim_set_option_value
